title: A Party of Workers or a Party of Intellectuals? Recruitment into Eastern European Communist Parties, 1945-1988
abstract: This paper provides an event-history analysis of recruitment into the Communist Party between 1945 and 1988 in Bulgaria, Hungary Poland, and the Czech and Slovak Socialist Republics. Despite evidence that gender discrimination in Party recruitment processes eased in some of these countries, women remained significantly less likely than men to enter the political arena via the Communist Party across the region. The findings also indicate that class background and parents' political affiliation figured less prominently in Party recruitment processes in Eastern Europe over time. Prior incumbency in a position of authority, including that of supervisor, not only had a positive effect on the odds of joining the Party but was greater than the effect of prior professional status, indicating that professionals were not especially likely to join the Party in Eastern Europe despite open efforts to recruit technically trained personnel. Education, however, had a large and stable effect on Party recruitment, with the absence of change in the coefficients for education across time suggesting that communist authorities imposed educational criteria on new recruits from their first years in power. Overall, the findings indicate the existence of two paths of entry into the Communist Parties of Eastern Europe, one through educational institutions and the other up the vertical chain of command within the workplace.
